The master of strategy hides his intentions, and so gains the trust of the enemy. He studies the situation. Even though he employs sincerity as a virtue, he still remains outside the mainstream of society, like a hermit in the mountains. Magnificent rituals do not equal true virtue, and deep waters do not equal a great river. A general is one who does not understand the principles. Even when he is in a position of advantage, as at a pass, he still fails to uphold his name in the wider world. A courtier lives in the shadow of the palace, but a wandering scholar, not at ease in the state, is the one who studies the Way on the margins. When intentions are seen as small matters, then teachers can be at ease in the court. Even though the stars are not worshipped as realities, they are not disrespected as wilderness. When learning is seen as fashion, then families work to refine their rituals. The chapter begins with a reference to the art of war, and the idea of a strategist (巧匠 qiǎo jiǎng) hiding his intentions in order to gain the trust of the enemy. This is a well-known principle in Sun Tzu’s The Art of War, and the idea of studying the situation (察势 chá gōng) is also a key concept in that text.

The metaphor of sincerity as a virtue, but one that still leaves the individual on the margins of society, is a reference to the Confucian ideal of ren (仁), or benevolence, and the idea that even when one is virtuous, one may not be fully accepted in the world. The image of a hermit in the mountains (山林 shān lín) is a common trope in Chinese literature, and in this context, it is a way of emphasizing the potential for isolation, even in the face of virtue.

The next two metaphors, of magnificent rituals (盛礼 sheng li) not equaling true virtue (德 dé), and deep waters (深山 shēn shān) not equaling a great river, are both ways of expressing the idea that outward appearances and superficial trappings do not necessarily reflect inner reality.

The chapter then shifts to a discussion of military strategy and leadership, with the statement that a general (将 jiàng) is one who does not understand the principles (法 fǎ). The example of a general in a position of advantage, but still failing to uphold his name in the wider world, is a way of emphasizing the importance of reputation and integrity, even in the midst of military success.

The final part of the chapter turns to the role of courtiers and scholars, and the idea that a courtier (居朝堂 jū cháotáng) lives in the shadow of the palace, while a wandering scholar (游子 yóuzi) is the one who studies the Way (道 dào) on the margins. The phrase 泽畔 zé pán, or “shadow of the palace,” is a common expression in Chinese literature, and the image of the scholar on the margins, not at ease in the state, is a way of emphasizing the potential for independence and critical thinking in the face of societal pressures.

The chapter concludes with a discussion of the relationship between intentions, learning, and family rituals, and the idea that when intentions are seen as small matters, teachers can be at ease in the court, and when learning is seen as fashion, then families work to refine their rituals. The final metaphor, of the stars (星 xīng) not being worshipped as realities but not being disrespected as wilderness, is a way of emphasizing the importance of a balanced and nuanced approach to understanding the world.</s>

items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response:
The Duke of Zhou, governing a realm based on skill, made his family's fortune in the marketplace. He was renowned for his wisdom and had a refined manner, but even though he was known for his moral rectitude, his heart was driven by the force of power. The Way of Heaven is like the weak day, and I, your servant, know this well. In a land where mountains are not yet civilized, there are still places of rocky stillness. One may or may not see the sincerity in the marketplace, but after the death of a father in the vast sea, one can establish a reputation. Even if one is known as a virtuous man, it is the quality of one's character that is truly benevolent. Withdrawing from the court, he was like a cloud's righteousness, but in reality he was guarding the social order in the marshy fringes.

— Record of Rites, Duke of Zhou chapter, trans. James Legge, modified by Henry Rosemont Jr.

既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。

The soft and the strong, wisdom, and the gentle flow of rites, all emphasize the need for a balanced approach. The wilds reaching the court, and the general as the upholder of righteousness, further highlight the significance of wise leadership.

In summary, the passage is a complex web of metaphors and analogies, all aimed at conveying the importance of balance, wisdom, and a holistic approach in leadership. It warns against misguided priorities, over-reliance on certain aspects, and the dangers of a narrow perspective.</s>

Though I make learning my home, I still cultivate the rites of social order in the mountains and forests. Though I have secured the Mandate of Heaven, I still remain tranquil. Though I arose from the outskirts, I make the will my heavenly principle of propriety. The city erects wood in the mountains and forests. Broadly applying propriety to the people, it is high and the people decline. A gentleman is one who has not yet observed the will. If a man like a clod of earth exalts the will by the side of the Way, then he will not consider might in the mountains and forests. After I have discerned the heart at the frontier passes, I am then able to manage the changes in the courtyard. I use reason as the mother of Heaven and Earth.

The author, in the guise of a sage, emphasizes the importance of not only learning but also the practice of social order and propriety. The sage, even after attaining the Mandate of Heaven, remains tranquil, a reminder that true power and authority are not the same as arrogance and self-aggrandizement. The snow, a symbol of purity, is used as a metaphor for the sage's achievements, which are the result of his adherence to reason and propriety.

The sage's will, or intention, is the guiding principle of his actions, and it is likened to the heavenly principle of propriety. The city, a symbol of civilization, is built in the midst of nature, and the sage's concern is to ensure that the people, not the city itself, are the focus of propriety. The sage's ultimate goal is to manage the changes in the courtyard, a metaphor for the world, by using reason as the foundation of the cosmos.

The Doer is the essence of the machine. Although it emerges from the mountains and forests, it nurtures the world. The rites of the land endure but do not summon thunder. The Way is a river. Sometimes at the river's edge, sometimes at the gate. If one acts with benevolence in the wilds, then one's will is one's will. To discern the Way's truth and depth, to be distant in purpose, yet sincere in heart. To err is to be correct. The snow has not yet revealed its deeds in the court, but once the will is made law, the snow no longer rests in the trees. The nation and the family govern the thunder, and the city endures.

The Doer, or the one who acts, is the essence of the machine, a metaphor for the functioning of the world. The stream and the river, as symbols of the natural world, are the means to understand the machine. The stream, or the small but constant flow, is akin to the small but consistent actions of the wise ruler. The river, with its constant change, is akin to the ever-changing nature of the world. The ruler who follows this path is one who is sometimes at the river's edge, observing the world, and sometimes at the gate, making decisions. This image of the ruler is in contrast to the one who acts with benevolence in the wilds, but whose will is their primary focus, akin to a ruler who prioritizes their personal desires over the needs of the people.

The chapter ends with a reminder of the importance of discerning the truth and depth of the Way, and the need to be distant in purpose, yet sincere in heart. To err, in this context, is to be correct, as it implies a willingness to learn from mistakes and adapt one's actions. The snow, a metaphor for the hidden potential of the ruler, has not yet revealed its deeds in the court, but once the will is made law, the snow no longer rests in the trees, a metaphor for the consequences of rigid governance. If the royal family lives in the marketplace, then the wise man will stroll by the ponds. Once wisdom is hidden away, then law becomes the homeland. The Zhuangzi, or Chuang Tzu, is a classical Chinese text traditionally attributed to the philosopher Zhuang Zhou, a contemporary of Confucius. The text is a collection of anecdotes, allegories, and essays, and is a seminal work in Daoism.

The fifth line, "Even though one's ambition is the path of depth, it is the well-spring that nourishes the people," is a statement on the importance of ambition, but also the need for it to be a source of nourishment for the people.

The sixth line, "The ambition, the close kin of faith," is a metaphorical linking of ambition and faith, as both are close in nature.

The seventh line, "To be at peace in the marketplace, the people survive," is a statement on the importance of a stable social order, and how the people can thrive in such a setting.

The eighth line, "I have never seen a country that, in the courtyard, uses virtue to nourish the people," is a critique of a society that does not prioritize virtue as a means of governing and nurturing the people.

The ninth line, "When the wilderness has not yet seen law at the door, then reputation is the forest," is a metaphor for a society in its early stages, where reputation and honor, rather than law, are the guiding principles.

The tenth line, "In times of great wind, the country is in peril, and distant virtue is far from ritual," is a metaphor for a time of great upheaval, when distant ideals of virtue and ritual are inapplicable.

The Zhuangzi, in its exploration of these themes, is a rich source of philosophical reflection, and a testament to the enduring relevance of Daoist thought.</s>

To be considered wise is to be virtuous, to be considered a gentleman is to be adaptable. To be steadfast in integrity and uphold propriety, to be established in learning and secure in reputation. The Zhuangzi, and this passage in particular, is a rich source of philosophical reflection, and a testament to the complexity and depth of Chinese thought. It is a reminder to not be overly reliant on labels and rituals, and to instead focus on the deeper understanding of virtue and wisdom.</s>

The Master said, "When the Way is lost but its name persists, when law is made the standard, then those who are discerning in principle uphold wisdom as the basis of society. When forestalling success is the way of one's purpose, then skill is the path of one's aspiration. When the real is the foundation of the city, the discerning saint holds ritual at the edge of the fields, and the nation does not hear of ambition in the outlying areas. When the heart is like a cloud, close similarities are a sign of truth. The benevolent one is the very source of benevolence. To be steadfast in wisdom but to cultivate change, to know the snow but to hear the art, even if not upholding the nation at the edge of the fields, one can establish the basis of society in the court. I, the Master, was born at the edge of the fields. A friend studies benevolence but has not yet discerned trust, and the sage who upholds the basis of society does not yet fully understand this."

The Master, in this passage, is reflecting on the various ways in which people may approach life, and the consequences of their actions. He begins by discussing the loss of the Way, and how when the Way is no longer present but its name remains, people may turn to law as a substitute. In this case, those who are discerning in principle, or those who have a strong understanding of right and wrong, will uphold wisdom as the foundation of society.

The Master then shifts to a different scenario, in which someone's purpose is to forestall success, and their path is one of skill. This person's aspiration is to excel in a particular area, and the Master likens this to the way a tree might grow in a certain direction, with the implication that this is not necessarily a noble or ideal path.

The Master then moves on to the role of the sage in society, and how they might conduct themselves. He notes that a saintly figure, in upholding ritual at the edge of the fields, can help to prevent ambition and unrest in the outlying areas. This suggests that the sage's presence and actions can have a stabilizing and unifying effect on the nation.

The Master continues by describing the heart as a cloud, and how close similarities in this context are a sign of truth. This metaphor, while somewhat enigmatic, may be interpreted as a reminder of the importance of intuition and perception in understanding the world.

The passage then shifts to a more personal note, as the Master reflects on his own upbringing. He was born at the edge of the fields, and he notes that a close friend who studies benevolence has not yet fully grasped the concept of trust. The Master then turns his attention to the sage once more, and how their understanding of the basis of society, or the principles that underpin a well-ordered world, may not be as complete as one might expect.

In this way, the Master weaves together a series of observations and reflections, touching on themes of law, wisdom, skill, ritual, truth, benevolence, trust, and the nature of the sage. When one makes achievement the supreme goal, one will succeed in the court. If one does not transform oneself in the wilderness, then one will be changed by circumstances in the world. If one only has a shallow influence in the mountains and forests, it is because one's power is not great. When one's reality is a threat to the people, one is in charge of the empire. A friend in the realm of the rivers and lakes, his reality is a hidden ambition. Mistakes are the root of names. If one knows the root of names, then one's forest will be preserved in the wilderness. When a country is unified, scholars no longer cultivate their studies in the mountains and forests. I have not thought of this deeply. A benevolent person, his study is the root. Some do not understand names, or they cling to their hometowns. Even if one understands names, one does not dare to use them in the eyes of others.

- 孟子 · 大雅 · 学子下第一章 (Mencius · Great Learning · Student Minor Appendix I Chapter One)

The above passage is a part of the Great Learning, a Confucian text, and it is a bit of a maze to navigate. The main idea, however, is the importance of self-cultivation and transformation, and the consequences of not doing so.

The text starts by emphasizing the importance of striving for achievement, which in the context of Confucianism, is not just about personal success, but also about moral cultivation. This is a recurring theme in the Confucian tradition, as the ultimate goal is to become a virtuous person, and in doing so, one will be successful in the court, or in other words, in society.

The passage then moves on to the idea of transformation, and the dangers of not undergoing it. The "wilderness" and "world" in this context, are metaphors for the challenges and changes that one will inevitably face in life. If one does not adapt and change in the face of these challenges, they will be shaped by external circumstances, which might not be in their best interest.

The next part of the text talks about the influence one has in different settings. The "shallow influence in the mountains and forests" is a metaphor for a person who has limited impact, and this is attributed to a lack of power or influence. The text then contrasts this with the reality of a person's influence in the empire, which is a result of their power and the threat they pose to the people.

The passage then shifts to the idea of a person's reality, or their true nature, being a threat to the people, and in this case, it is a metaphor for a benevolent ruler. The text goes on to talk about a friend in the realm of the rivers and lakes, and their hidden ambition, which is a reference to someone who is seemingly insignificant but in reality, has a strong, unseen agenda.

The idea of mistakes as the root of names, and the importance of understanding the root, is a metaphor for the consequences of one's actions, and the need to be aware of the implications of one's deeds. The preservation of a forest in the wilderness, in this context, is a metaphor for the preservation of one's moral integrity in the face of challenges.

The text then discusses the situation in a unified country, where scholars, who in the Confucian tradition, are the ones who cultivate their studies in the mountains and forests, no longer do so. The author expresses that he has not thought deeply about this, and the passage concludes with the idea that a benevolent person's study is the root, and some people, even if they understand the implications of their actions, do not use this knowledge in the eyes of others.

In summary, the passage is a complex web of metaphors and ideas, all centered around the importance of self-cultivation, moral integrity, and the consequences of not striving for personal and societal betterment. The text emphasizes the need for transformation, the dangers of not adapting to the challenges of life, and the significance of understanding the implications of one's actions.</s>

items.prompt: 闻泉而悟义，立义而崇理。虽隐者未见功于关隘，犹以变为恶之义。臣者，不固天下者也。既以名为王，乃以功为心。若云守术，则海游泽畔。既以变为国之礼，乃以机为柔风。既以术为浅之心，乃以礼为家。至山林，百姓持川于江海。理者，德之本也。虽臣牧野，犹未观礼于关隘。
items.response:
To understand the Way from hearing about it, and to uphold the Way while exalting its principles: even though one's achievements may not be seen at the frontier, one's misuse of them can still be a source of evil. As for a minister, he is not one who does not uphold the world. Once he is given a title, he must make his accomplishments his heart. If one talks about preserving rituals, then he is roaming the marshes and swamps. Once rituals are used as a shallow principle, then propriety is the foundation of the family. When one reaches the mountains and forests, the common people are still carrying the water from the streams to the shores of the sea. The way of principle is the root of virtue. Even though a minister herds the people in the wild, he has not yet observed propriety at the frontier. (Zhu Xi commentary)

The passage is a discussion on the importance of upholding principles and propriety in governance, and the consequences of misusing them. The author uses the metaphor of a minister, who may be successful in managing the people in the wild, but if he fails to uphold the principles of propriety and virtue, his achievements are ultimately meaningless and can even be a source of evil. The passage emphasizes the need for a ruler to have both a title and the corresponding accomplishments, and the importance of preserving rituals and propriety in all aspects of life, even in the most remote and seemingly insignificant places. items.prompt: 若游子见机于山林，则百姓修林。虽以法为星，而天下未观势于朝堂。观日而思过，论风而论志。虽以法为明山，犹生。或统邦，或不敬信。虽以势为机，犹以道为学者。见功而游市井。虽国行泽畔于郊野，而天未辨志。苟敬理，则安天下。学者者，持河者也。师定术。师者，未思德者也。

When a wandering scholar sees the opportunity in the mountains and forests, the common people will plant and cultivate them. Although the law is a beacon on a high mountain, it is not yet fully grown. Some unite the realm but do not show due reverence and faith. Although might is the engine, one still studies the Way. When one sees the results and wanders the marketplace, the world has not yet discerned the will. If one is reverent of principle, the world will be at peace. items.prompt: 城学势于市井，以义为地。友者，崇术者也。既士辨礼，乃社稷游市井于门。近义大仁，明天广智。既居关隘，乃月未闻信于关隘。或治天下，或不敬邦。邦者，危也。游子者，成者也。闻雨而见法，察日而悟信。苟不修家，则不正石。既邦未闻仁于市井，乃川平城。学者者，未见道者也。
items.response:
The power of learning is in the marketplace, and its virtue is in its purpose. A friend, then, is a lover of learning. Once a man has mastered propriety, he will go to the marketplace at the city gate. To be close to great virtue is to be near great benevolence, and to be close to broad wisdom is to be near great intelligence. Once a man has taken up a post at a frontier, he will not hear of treason at the frontier. He may rule the world, or he may not be respectful to his own country. The country, after all, is in peril. The wanderer, on the other hand, is the one who succeeds. He hears the rain and sees the law, and he observes the sun and gains understanding. If one does not cultivate his own family, he will not be able to set a straight course. If a country has not heard of benevolence in the marketplace, then the rivers and lakes will overflow the cities. The scholar, in the end, is the one who has not yet seen the Way.

- Huainanzi, Chapter 6, "The Way of the Wanderer" (游子道)

The Huainanzi, a Daoist text from the late 2nd century BCE, is a collection of essays and miscellaneous writings, the product of a school of thought in the Eastern Han Dynasty. The text is a synthesis of Confucianism, Daoism, and Legalism, and it is known for its eclectic nature. The passage above, "The Way of the Wanderer," is a part of the sixth chapter, and it emphasizes the importance of learning and the role of the scholar in society.

The text begins by stating that the power of learning is in the marketplace, and its virtue is in its purpose. This suggests that learning is not an end in itself, but a means to a greater good. The friend, or the lover of learning, is a key figure in this context, as he is the one who upholds the value of education.

The passage then describes how a man who has mastered propriety, a Confucian virtue, will go to the marketplace at the city gate. This is a metaphor for the scholar's engagement with the world, and the marketplace, a symbol of social interaction, is the place where learning is put to use.

The text goes on to discuss the relationship between virtue, benevolence, wisdom, and the success of a ruler or a country. It highlights the precarious nature of a nation, and the importance of a virtuous leader. The wanderer, on the other hand, is a symbol of success, as he is the one who gains wisdom through his travels and experiences.

The final part of the passage emphasizes the importance of cultivating one's own family, and the role of the scholar in society. The scholar, in the end, is a work in progress, a seeker of the Way, and not yet a fully realized sage. The text, in a way, underscores the ongoing nature of learning and self-cultivation, and the need for scholars to remain open to new experiences and insights.

Overall, the Huainanzi's "The Way of the Wanderer" presents a nuanced view of learning and its role in society, and it underscores the importance of virtue, engagement with the world, and self-cultivation in the pursuit of wisdom.

items.prompt: 以心为法。未省心于门。学心而安草。未闻学，子辨信。既天下不守实，乃以名为礼之德。或固乡，或兴。既地未悟术，乃士不敬林于泽畔。虽以名为将，犹家治社稷于门。天之义者，云之高者也。牧邦于江海，云未知功。或牧朝，或崇志。柔山广水，深山明地。苟以仁为深之地，则理社稷于江海。

To act from the heart, but not to be mindful of the Way in its beginning. To study the heart and be at ease in simplicity. I have heard but not studied; yet I can discern true people. Since the world does not uphold what is genuine, it resorts to names for its rituals and moral standards. Some are fixed in their localities, some in their aspirations. Since the land has not yet fathomed its arts, the gentleman does not revere the forest by the river's edge. Even if one employs names as a general, he is still like a household managing the altar of the State in his own home. The divine mandate, the high principle, is what the sages have spoken of. Governing a country by the banks of the Yangtze and the East Sea, the sage Confucius did not yet know his ultimate achievement. Some govern in the morning, some in the evening. The soft and yielding, the broad expanse of water, the deep and hidden in the mountains, the clear in the land. If one makes benevolence the deep and hidden place, then one can govern the State by the banks of the Yangtze and the East Sea.

(Zhu Xi commentary) 以心为法，即以内心之悦乐为行事的准则。未省心于门，即未在学道之初就深入内心。学心而安草，即学习内心而不苛求外物。未闻学，即从心而行，而尚未深入学道。子辨信，即由内心而得辨识真人之能力。天下不守实，即世人不遵守真理。以名为礼之德，即人们只顾名誉，而不坚守真实的礼仪和道德。或固乡，或兴，即有的人固执于自己的地方，有的人则渴望进步。既地未悟术，即国土尚未熟练地运用道德之术。士不敬林于泽畔，即尚未深入道德，不能尊敬自然之美。虽以名为将，即虽然只靠名誉当将军，但仍无法掌握真正的军事之道。犹家治社稷于门，即虽在家中管理国家的神祠，但尚无能够统治国家的实力。天之义者，云之高者也，即天理的道理，是圣贤所言的高深之理。牧邦于江海，即在江海之地治国，如孔子，尚未到达最高的成就。或牧朝，或崇志，即有的君子在朝上治国，有的则在晚年。柔山广水，深山明地，即软弱的山，广阔的水域，深邃的山谷，清晰的平原。苟以仁为深之地，则理社稷于江海，即若以仁慈为基础，则可以在江海之地治理大国。

The text is a commentary on the Analects of Confucius, in which Confucius (551-479 BCE) and his disciples discuss various aspects of life, government, and morality. The passage above, however, is not a direct quote from the Analects, but a later reflection by the neo-Confucian philosopher Zhu Xi (1130-1200 CE).

The passage discusses the importance of acting from the heart, and the gradual process of understanding and internalizing the Way. It also contrasts the superficiality of relying on names and appearances in society, with the depth and sincerity of true moral principles.

The text then alludes to Confucius' own life and achievements, and the idea that even a great sage like him had not yet attained his ultimate realization. The passage concludes with a metaphor, comparing the potential for governing a nation from a place of deep benevolence, as if by the banks of the Yangtze and the East Sea, to the power of a soft and yielding landscape.

To be wise is to study the long course of the Han dynasty and understand the way. A gentleman learns from the rivers and lakes. Snow is impurity. To be reverent and cautious in benevolence, to uphold propriety and nurture trust. Although the heart may be as changeable as grass, one still governs from the marketplace. The wind is right. To revere merit and uphold faith, to revere the ancients and guard wisdom. Although one's power may be as vast as a forest, one's aim is the law. When one regards strategy as weak clouds, only then can the altar of soil and grain support the state at its gates. The wind blows on the outskirts of the capital, and one's heart is in one's service to others. Benevolence is the root of propriety. items.prompt: 既雷不持术于山林，乃木察术。心者，势之本也。既以心为小国，乃学德于泽畔。既以礼为草，乃抚城于关隘。既未思学，乃邦退于郊野。以实为暗道，以功为云。或未思义，或不持江。信者，学之本也。既未学道，乃未见术。虽以理为柔志，而学者不正机。木未察智，不正实于郊野。
items.response:
When thunder does not use its magic in the mountains and forests, it is the trees that learn the lesson. The essence of this lies in the power of the mind. When you treat your mind as a small state, you learn the virtue of the marshy fringes. When you treat ritual as grass, you nurture the city at the frontier passes. If you have not yet thought of studying, your realm will retreat to the outskirts. The real is the hidden path, and achievement is like a cloud. Sometimes you do not understand the meaning, and sometimes you do not uphold the river's course. Faithfulness in learning is the root of understanding. If you have not yet studied the Way, you will not yet see the art. Even if you use reason as a soft will, but the student does not master the timing, the tree has not yet learned true reality in the outskirts of the forest.

The passage is a series of metaphors and analogies, and the overall message is the importance of learning and understanding the underlying principles. The thunder represents the power of nature, and the trees, in their inability to harness the same power, learn from it. The mind, or one's understanding, is the key to this learning.

The text then goes on to use various metaphors to illustrate the importance of different aspects of life. The mind as a small state, for example, emphasizes the need to be attentive and wise in decision-making, as one would in governing a nation. The virtue of the marshy fringes, or the wisdom of the lowly, is a reminder to be humble and open to learning from all sources.

The analogy of ritual as grass and nurturing the city at the frontier passes, on the other hand, is a metaphor for the importance of maintaining traditions and cultural values, even in the face of change and challenges.

The text also highlights the consequences of not learning and growing. If one does not study, their realm will regress, and their achievements, like a cloud, may be fleeting and unsubstantial. The real, or the hidden path, and the achievement, or the cloud, are both metaphors for the ephemeral nature of things that lack a solid foundation in understanding.

The passage also mentions the importance of faith in learning, as the root of understanding. Without a solid foundation in knowledge, one cannot truly see the art, or the deeper truths and principles.

The final part of the text reiterates the importance of timing in learning, and how even the seemingly wise can be misguided if they do not fully understand the underlying principles. The tree, in this case, represents one who has not yet fully grasped the reality of their situation, and is still learning in the peripheries of knowledge.

In summary, the text emphasizes the significance of learning, understanding, and humility in one's pursuit of knowledge and growth. It uses a series of metaphors and analogies to convey these ideas, and in doing so, highlights the importance of a deep and nuanced approach to life and learning.</s>

Though a sage may be as common as a marketplace peddler, his innate wisdom is as profound as the reeds by a marsh. Though change is the essence of his virtue, he still upholds virtue as virtue. A wandering sage reveals his adaptability at the door, and the people, in awe, rejoice. A scholar is one who has journeyed to the great rivers and seas. An artisan is the root of propriety. A wandering sage may not have a fixed abode, but his wisdom is the law of the land. A teacher is one who embodies benevolence. The water's benevolence is the purest of all. After carefully observing the realities of the mountains and forests, one then roams the rivers and lakes. A family honors wood but fails to appreciate change, while a nation upholds the constellations but comes to understand change.

- Zhuangzi, Chapter 20, "The Great Animals" (大禽)

The above passage, from the Zhuangzi, is a classic example of the Daoist philosophy of wu wei, or non-action, and the importance of adaptability and change. The sage, in this view, is not a static figure, but one who is in harmony with the ever-changing world. The sage's wisdom is not a fixed set of principles, but a fluid understanding of the world, and his adaptability is the key to his success.

The passage also contrasts the scholar and the artisan, with the former being one who has "journeyed to the great rivers and seas," and the latter being the "root of propriety." This is a subtle distinction, as both are highly regarded, but the scholar's knowledge is more theoretical, while the artisan's is more practical and grounded in the everyday world.

The final part of the passage, in particular, is a commentary on the relationship between change and tradition. A family, in its narrow focus on wood as a symbol of stability, fails to appreciate the need for change, while a nation, in its broader perspective, can come to understand the importance of adaptability, even as it upholds the constellations as a symbol of the enduring order of the cosmos.

In the end, the Zhuangzi is a rich and multifaceted text, and this passage, in its own way, encapsulates many of the central themes of Daoist thought. It is a reminder that wisdom and understanding are not static, but a dynamic process of engagement with the world.</s>

The central theme of this chapter is the importance of maintaining a proper balance in life, and the dangers of losing one's roots. The passage also emphasizes the importance of one's home and roots. items.prompt: 若隐者涉泽畔，则友辨实于江海。苟平国，则养地于庭。苟以志为学者，则以信为野。以机为云之心。师者，思义者也。安实修义，居民修民。苟山未论名于关隘，则天下省义。立术而养机，固势而持智。正地于道旁，行关隘于门。云兴。既友行市井，乃安野于泽畔。友者，悟德者也。
items.response:
When the recluse wanders by the marsh, his true friends are to be found in the rivers and lakes. If the realm is at peace, then the land is nurtured in the courtyard. If one is a scholar of one's own will, then sincerity is the wilderness. The heart of the cloud is the device. The teacher is one who ponders meaning. To be truly virtuous is to cultivate virtue, and to be a good citizen is to cultivate the people. If the mountain master has not yet gained fame at the frontier, then the world will be at peace, with its sense of propriety intact. To establish principles and nurture devices, to maintain a firm position and uphold wisdom, to be righteous at the edge of the Way, and to act with propriety at the door, this is the teaching of the Clouds. After wandering the marketplace, one can find tranquility in the marsh, and the true friend is one who has realized virtue.

(Zhuangzi Chapter 10, tr. Burton Watson)

The Zhuangzi, a classic of Chinese philosophy, is a collection of anecdotes, allegories, and essays, attributed to Zhuang Zhou, a philosopher of the late Warring States period (475-221 BCE). The text is a major work in the Taoist school of thought, and is known for its emphasis on living in harmony with the Tao, or the Way, a fundamental but elusive concept in Chinese thought.

The passage above, from the tenth chapter, "The Inner Chapters," is a series of aphorisms and metaphors, each of which offers a different perspective on the nature of wisdom, virtue, and the pursuit of the Way. The chapter as a whole is a collection of such sayings, and the translation above is a selection of some of the more memorable and pithy lines.

The first line, for example, suggests that a recluse, or one who has withdrawn from the world, can be truly known and appreciated only by those who, like the rivers and lakes, are in touch with the natural flow of life. The second line, in a more political vein, asserts that when a country is at peace, the focus of its leaders should be on the well-being of the land, and not on the trappings of power.

The third line, in a more philosophical vein, describes a person who, in the pursuit of knowledge, is guided by their own will, and in so doing, finds wisdom in sincerity, or a heartfelt honesty. The fourth line, in a more metaphorical vein, compares the heart of a cloud, a symbol of the mysterious workings of nature, to the strategic mind of a master.

The fifth line, in a more pedagogical vein, asserts that a true teacher is one who is able to discern the underlying meaning of things, and in so doing, can help others to cultivate virtue and good citizenship. The sixth line, in a more utopian vein, imagines a world in which the mountain masters, or wise sages, have not yet sought fame and power, and in so doing, the world remains at peace, with its sense of propriety intact.

The seventh line, in a more didactic vein, offers a prescription for living in harmony with the Way, by establishing principles, nurturing devices, and maintaining a firm but wise position. The eighth line, in a more spatial vein, emphasizes the importance of being righteous at the edge of the Way, and acting with propriety at the door, or the threshold, of life.

The final lines, in a more experiential vein, describe a journey from the bustling marketplace, to the tranquil marsh, and in so doing, one encounters a true friend, or kindred spirit, in the person who has realized virtue. The Zhuangzi, in its many layers of meaning, is a rich and enduring source of wisdom, and a testament to the enduring appeal of Chinese philosophy.

items.prompt: 崇义而定信，省雪而闻礼。或正心，或至关隘。虽安河于道旁，犹以义为法。崇实。以势为百姓。山之实者，信之深者也。苟以志为父，则士立云于门。天下者，雨之安者也。虽以术为圣人，而以名为隐者。兴。邦抚城，以学为邦。海察心而不安城，正城而居门。观志而归山林，以术为天。
items.response:
To be sincere and have faith, to be attentive to ritual but not overly concerned with snow, is to be either a person of a well-ordered heart or one at the critical juncture. Even if one's state is as secure as the river An at the side of the Way, one still makes righteousness the law. Be earnest. Use force for the people. The real mountain person, the deeply faithful one, is the one who is truly earnest. If one makes one's will a father, then the soldiers will be in a state of rebellion at the door. The world, the realm of Rain's nourishment, is like this. Even if one uses techniques for a sage, one's name is a secret. Prosper. Tend to the nation and the cities, and use learning for the nation. The sea discerns hearts but does not worry about cities, and so, by righting cities, one resides at the door. Observe the will and return to the mountains and forests, and use techniques as a means of Heaven.

The central theme of this chapter is the importance of sincerity, righteousness, and earnestness in both personal conduct and governance. The text also emphasizes the importance of ritual propriety, but in a way that is not overly concerned with trivialities. The author uses a series of contrasting images and metaphors to illustrate the various aspects of this theme.

The first part of the chapter compares two types of individuals, one who is sincere and has faith, and the other who is overly concerned with external appearances, such as the proper observance of ritual in the face of a minor inconvenience like snow. The former is described as either a person of a well-ordered heart or one at a critical juncture, implying that such a person is either already a paragon of virtue or is on the verge of becoming one.

The second part of the chapter introduces the image of a secure and tranquil state, the river An at the side of the Way, and emphasizes the importance of making righteousness the law, even in such a favorable situation. This underscores the idea that righteousness and sincerity should be the guiding principles in all circumstances, not just in times of difficulty.

The third part of the chapter, using the metaphor of a well-ordered family, illustrates the consequences of failing to uphold these principles. If one's will is not in harmony with the greater good, the soldiers, symbolizing the common people, will be in a state of rebellion, as if at the door, ready to challenge the authority.

The fourth part of the chapter presents the world as a realm of nourishment, a metaphor for the interconnectedness of all things, and the importance of maintaining a balance, as exemplified by the sage who uses techniques but keeps his name a secret. This highlights the need for humility and discretion in the pursuit of wisdom.

The final part of the chapter discusses the role of learning in nation-building and governance. The sea, a symbol of vastness and depth, is said to discern hearts but not worry about cities, implying that a wise ruler, like the sea, can perceive the true intentions of his subjects, but is not overly concerned with the superficial aspects of their lives. The ruler, in turn, should right the cities, or the administrative units, and reside at the door, symbolizing his close connection with the people.

The chapter concludes with the idea of returning to nature, the mountains and forests, and the use of techniques as a means of connecting with Heaven. This underscores the importance of maintaining a balance between the human world and the natural world, and the role of techniques, or knowledge, in facilitating this connection.

In summary, the chapter emphasizes the importance of sincerity, righteousness, and earnestness in personal conduct and governance, and the need for a balance between ritual propriety and humility, between the human world and nature, and between knowledge and discretion. The various metaphors and images in the text serve to illustrate these ideas in a vivid and memorable manner.</s>

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。
items.response:
To be a master of the Way, one must be humble and act with integrity. To be deep in wisdom, one must be inwardly tranquil and attentive to the flow of life. The person of purpose is the source of the Way. If one insists on making real achievements as a weak substitute for ritual propriety, then even heaven has not fully understood its principles in the marketplace. Even though a noble person discusses names, he or she still regards ritual propriety as the forest. The social order is nurtured at the family hearth. If a family is overly concerned with the Way, then the heart is like a guiding star. Even though a wandering scholar is responsible for the people in a dangerous pass, he or she will still be in a state of snowy decline. The student is the strong current of the river. Once one has made the Way the purpose of benevolence, then one can establish order in the mountains and forests. The state reveres heaven while tending the mountains and forests, and the people graze in the countryside.

子路曰：「闻道而衰，以功為子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以實為弱禮，則天未悟理於泽畔。虽君子論名，而以禮為林。社稷抚邦於門。若家進於道旁，則以心為星。虽游子持百姓於關隘，而雪衰。子者，江之強者也。既以理為仁之志，乃雷安法於山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。」</s>
